perturbation.original_text: In this document, the following terms will have the following definitions: (a)“Third person” includes, but is not limited to, any person, company, association, organization, union, corporation, and any other entity or individual
perturbation.changed_text: In this document, the following terms will have the following definitions: (a)“Third person” includes only natural persons, excluding companies, associations, organizations, unions, corporations, and any other entity or individual
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of "Third person" is broad. By limiting it to only natural persons, it contradicts the standard legal interpretation that includes various entities. This creates ambiguity about who is covered by the confidentiality agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Interpretation Act of New Brunswick defines "person" broadly to include corporations and other entities, not just natural persons. The modified text contradicts this established legal definition, potentially invalidating clauses of the agreement that rely on the definition of "Third person."

perturbation.justification: The changed text narrows the definition of "Third person" to only natural persons, explicitly excluding companies, associations, organizations, unions, corporations, and any other entity or individual, while the Law Explanation states this contradicts the Interpretation Act of New Brunswick, which defines "person" broadly to include corporations and other entities. The modified text thus deviates from the standard legal definition, which could invalidate parts of the agreement that use the term "Third person."

perturbation.original_text: Confidential information” refers to and encompasses any information acquired or received by the potential Franchisee from or concerning the Franchisor, including, without limiting the generality of the foregoing: i.any information concerning the Franchise, even if this information is known or available to the general public
perturbation.changed_text: "Confidential information” refers to and encompasses any information acquired or received by the potential Franchisee from or concerning the Franchisor, including only trade secrets which are of an economic value.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition includes information known to the general public. The modification limits it to trade secrets of economic value, contradicting the initial expansive scope of the term, and potentially nullifying the entire agreement because trade secrets have a particular legal definition.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets Act defines trade secrets narrowly. By limiting "Confidential information" to this definition, the contract excludes other types of information the Franchisor may have intended to protect, creating a conflict and limiting the enforceability of the agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: statute | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you statute A statute is a law enacted by a legislature. Statutes are also called acts, such as the Civil Rights Act of 1964 or the Sarbanes-Oxley Act . Federal laws must be passed by both houses of Congress, the House of Representative and the Senate, and then usually require approval from the President before they can take effect. As explained by the Library of Congress , enacted federal statutes are published multiple times. First, each individual law is published as a “slip law.” Then, all of the slip laws for each session of Congress are published together as “session laws.” Finally, all laws that are of a “general and permanent nature” are eventually compiled into the United States Code , and also the Revised Statutes of the United States. State statutes can be found through the LII's page on Constitutions, Statutes, and Codes . Readers can also attempt to find state statutes organized by topic: Topical Index: State Statutes . [Last reviewed in May of 2020 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex CIVICS wex definitions government legal education and practice statutes Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of "Confidential information" to only include trade secrets of economic value, while the original text had a broader scope including information known to the general public. The Perturbation Explanation notes this can nullify the agreement because trade secrets have a particular legal definition. The Law Explanation states this change limits the enforceability of the agreement, supporting that a contradiction exists. The provided snippet about statutes explains that a statute is a law enacted by legislature, but does not clarify the definition of trade secrets which is the central issue in determining whether the revised clause contradicts the original intent. This contradiction creates a misaligned terminology legal contradiction because it deviates from legally recognized definitions, specifically potentially trade secret law, and therefore makes the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Return of confidential information: All documents, letters, reports, protocols, notes, journals, drawings, plans, maps, sketches, spreadsheets, data, and any other written material, correspondence, archive, or e-mail (including any electronic material) containing confidential information, as well as any copies of these materials, must be returned by the potential Franchisee upon written request to do so from the Franchisor or at any time at the Franchisor’s discretion.
perturbation.changed_text: Return of confidential information: All documents, letters, reports, protocols, notes, journals, drawings, plans, maps, sketches, spreadsheets, data, and any other written material, correspondence, archive, or e-mail (including any electronic material) containing confidential information, as well as any copies of these materials, must be returned by the potential Franchisee upon written request to do so from the Franchisor, unless such documents are required for compliance with applicable laws.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates unconditional return of confidential information. By adding the 'unless required for compliance with applicable laws' clause, it introduces a potential conflict because applicable laws may require destroying or storing documents which would contradict the requirement to return the documents to the franchisor.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act, SC 2000, c 5
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification could be interpreted as conflicting with PIPEDA, which in some cases requires organizations to retain information for specific periods. Adding 'unless required for compliance with applicable laws' creates uncertainty, making the obligation to return confidential information ambiguous and potentially unenforceable.

Marginal note: Accessing a computer system to collect personal information, etc. (3) Â Paragraphs 7(1)(a) to (d) and (2)(a) to (c.1) and the exception set out in clause 4.3 of Schedule 1 do not apply in respect of (a) Â the collection of personal information, through any means of telecommunication, if the collection is made by accessing a computer system or causing a computer system to be accessed in contravention of an Act of Parliament; or (b) Â the use of personal information that is collected in a manner described in paragraph (a). 2010, c. 23, s. 82 2015, c. 32, s. 26 Previous Version Marginal note: Prospective business transaction 7.2 Â (1) Â In addition to the circumstances set out in subsectionsÂ 7(2) and (3), for the purpose of clauseÂ 4.3 of ScheduleÂ 1, and despite the note that accompanies that clause, organizations that are parties to a prospective business transaction may use and disclose personal information without the knowledge or consent of the individual if (a) Â the organizations have entered into an agreement that requires the organization that receives the personal information (i) Â to use and disclose that information solely for purposes related to the transaction, (ii) Â to protect that information by security safeguards appropriate to the sensitivity of the information, and (iii) Â if the transaction does not proceed, to return that information to the organization that disclosed it, or destroy it, within a reasonable time; and (b) Â the personal information is necessary (i) Â to determine whether to proceed with the tran
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a condition ('unless required for compliance with applicable laws') that could potentially override the original requirement to return confidential information to the Franchisor. According to the Law Explanation, this could conflict with PIPEDA, which may require organizations to retain information for certain periods. The changed text creates ambiguity regarding the franchisee's obligation to return information, particularly if other laws mandate its retention or destruction. The original text mandates an unconditional return, while the changed text introduces a potential out that could render the original obligation unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: The present Agreement shall remain subject to the laws of the province of New Brunswick, the courts of the province of New Brunswick having sole jurisdiction in respect of any action or proceeding arising out of the present Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The present Agreement shall remain subject to the laws of the province of Ontario, the courts of the province of Ontario having sole jurisdiction in respect of any action or proceeding arising out of the present Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The contract specifies New Brunswick law and jurisdiction. Changing this to Ontario law and jurisdiction creates a conflict because the parties involved are from New Brunswick. This could result in a legal challenge regarding the proper venue and applicable law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Rules of Court under the Judicature Act, RSNB 2014, c 106
perturbation.law_explanation: Changing the governing law and jurisdiction from New Brunswick to Ontario could contradict New Brunswick's rules regarding jurisdiction over contracts formed within the province. Rule 6.01 outlines provisions for cases within New Brunswick, and the changed text undermines this by imposing Ontario jurisdiction.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies New Brunswick law and jurisdiction. The changed text replaces this with Ontario law and jurisdiction. The Perturbation Explanation states this could result in a legal challenge, especially since the parties involved are from New Brunswick. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that changing the governing law could contradict New Brunswick's rules regarding jurisdiction over contracts formed within the province. This aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction', where the deviation from legally recognized definitions makes the contract unenforceable in New Brunswick. While the provided law snippets are not directly relevant, the explanations clearly indicate a contradiction in jurisdictional law.
